content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Both Tom ___ Lisa enjoy cooking at home on weekends.
distractor_rationale: both…or is not a valid correlative pair; both always pairs with and
both…nor is not a valid correlative pair; nor belongs with neither
both…but is not a valid correlative pair; but expresses contrast, not parallel inclusion

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: You can have ___ tea or coffee — just let me know which one you prefer.
distractor_rationale: both…or is not a valid correlative pair; both must pair with and, and it means all options, not a choice between two
neither…or is not a valid correlative pair; neither pairs with nor and means not one and not the other
none is not a correlative conjunction and cannot pair with or to offer a choice

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Neither the soup ___ the salad was included in the set menu price.
distractor_rationale: neither…or is a very common learner error; neither must always pair with nor, not or
neither…and is not a valid correlative pair; and is used with both, not neither
neither…but is not a valid correlative pair; but expresses contrast and cannot complete neither

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The new sports centre is not only large ___ also very well equipped for professional training.
distractor_rationale: not only…and is not a valid correlative pair; the correct second element is but also
not only…or is not a valid correlative pair; or signals alternatives, not the additive emphasis of not only…but also
not only…nor is not a valid correlative pair; nor belongs in the neither…nor construction

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Neither the head teacher nor the school board ___ yet been told about the change to the timetable.
distractor_rationale: with neither…nor, the verb agrees with the closer subject (the school board), which is singular, so have is incorrect
had would form the past perfect, but the sentence uses yet and present context, requiring present perfect
were is a past simple plural form, which does not agree with the singular closer subject and uses the wrong tense

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Both the living room ___ the kitchen need to be repainted before the new tenants move in.
distractor_rationale: both…or is not a valid correlative pair; both must always be followed by and
both…nor is not a valid correlative pair; nor is the partner of neither, not both
both…as well is not a standard correlative structure; as well cannot replace and in this construction

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: You can ___ submit your application online or hand it in at the reception desk before Friday.
distractor_rationale: both…or is not a valid correlative pair; both pairs with and and cannot introduce a two-option choice here
neither…or is not a valid correlative pair, and neither would mean no option is available, contradicting the sentence
whether…or introduces indirect questions or conditions, not a direct instruction offering a choice of actions

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The project manager said that neither the budget ___ the deadline could be changed at such a late stage.
distractor_rationale: neither…or is the most frequent learner error with this structure; nor is the only grammatically valid partner for neither
neither…and is not a valid correlative pair; and is used with both to join two positive elements
neither…either is not a valid correlative pair; either belongs in the either…or construction

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The new employee is not only hardworking but ___ extremely reliable, which has impressed the whole team.
distractor_rationale: not only…but too is not a standard correlative pair; also is the required word to complete but also after not only
not only…but as well is non-standard; as well typically appears at the end of a clause and cannot replace also here
not only…but even changes the meaning to one of surprise or unexpectedness, breaking the parallel additive structure

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Neither the witnesses nor the defendant ___ present when the judge read out the final decision in the case.
distractor_rationale: with neither…nor, the verb agrees with the closer subject (the defendant), which is singular, making were incorrect
have been is present perfect, but the sentence describes a completed past event with no link to the present
are is present simple, which contradicts the past time reference of the judge reading out the final decision

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: You can pay ___ cash or by card at the checkout.
distractor_rationale: both requires 'and', not 'or', so 'both…or' is grammatically incorrect
neither requires 'nor' and expresses a negative meaning, but the sentence offers two valid options
whether introduces an indirect question and cannot link two parallel noun phrases as alternatives

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Both the coffee ___ the tea are included in the price of breakfast.
distractor_rationale: 'both…or' is grammatically incorrect; 'both' must be paired with 'and'
'both…nor' is not a standard correlative pair and produces a nonsensical structure
'both…but' is not a recognised correlative conjunction pattern in English

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Neither the heating ___ the hot water was working when the guests arrived at the hotel.
distractor_rationale: 'neither…or' is a classic learner error; 'neither' must be paired with 'nor', not 'or'
'neither…and' is not a valid correlative structure in English
'neither…but' does not form a recognised correlative conjunction pair

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The new assistant is not only organised but ___ very good at dealing with difficult customers.
distractor_rationale: 'not only…but even' is not the fixed correlative pair; 'also' is the required second element
'not only…but too' is not the standard correlative structure; 'too' cannot complete 'not only…but'
'as well' cannot directly follow 'but' to complete the 'not only…but also' correlative pair

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Neither the project manager nor the two junior designers ___ told about the change to the deadline before the meeting.
distractor_rationale: with 'neither…nor', the verb agrees with the closer subject ('the two junior designers', which is plural), so 'was' is incorrect
'are' is present tense, but the context ('before the meeting') places the event in the past, making present tense impossible
'have been' is present perfect, which cannot be used here because the specific past reference point ('before the meeting') requires simple past

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ Tom nor Sara was ready when the bus arrived.
distractor_rationale: Either requires or and does not express that both subjects are excluded; 'Either Tom nor Sara' is grammatically incorrect
Both requires and and expresses inclusion of both subjects, the opposite meaning needed here
Not only requires but also as its pair and cannot open a negative statement about two subjects this way

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: You can pay ___ by card or with cash at the checkout.
distractor_rationale: neither…nor signals that neither option is possible, contradicting the meaning that both payment methods are available
both…and connects two things that are both true simultaneously, but paying by card and with cash at the same time is not the intended meaning here
not only requires but also as its second part and does not pair with or

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ my brother and I enjoy cooking at home on weekends.
distractor_rationale: Either…or presents a choice between two options, not a statement that both people share the same enjoyment
Neither…nor states that neither person does something, which is the opposite of the intended meaning
Not only requires but also as its paired conjunction and cannot stand alone before a noun phrase here

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The new employee is not only hard-working ___ extremely organised in her daily tasks.
distractor_rationale: 'not only…but' without 'also' is incomplete in standard usage and fails to form the full correlative structure required here
'and also' does not complete the 'not only' correlative pair; 'not only…and also' is not a recognised correlative conjunction pattern
'or also' is not a grammatical correlative conjunction and creates a nonsensical structure after 'not only'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Neither the head teacher ___ any of the staff members had heard about the new policy before the meeting.
distractor_rationale: 'neither…or' is a classic learner error; the correct paired conjunction for neither is nor, not or
'neither…and' is not a correlative pair; and is used with both, not neither
'neither…but' is not a recognised correlative conjunction structure in English

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Both the kitchen ___ the bathroom need to be cleaned before our guests arrive this evening.
distractor_rationale: 'both…or' is a common learner error; both must be paired with and, not or
'both…nor' is incorrect; nor is the paired conjunction for neither, not both
'both…but' is not a recognised correlative conjunction; but is used for contrast, not coordination with both

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: You should bring ___ a warm jacket or a thick jumper, because the mountain weather can change quickly.
distractor_rationale: 'both…or' is ungrammatical; both pairs with and, so 'both a warm jacket or a thick jumper' is incorrect
'neither…or' is doubly wrong — neither pairs with nor and signals that neither item should be brought, contradicting the advice
'not only…or' is ungrammatical; not only pairs with but also, not with or

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Neither the project manager nor the junior developers ___ informed about the deadline change until the last minute.
distractor_rationale: When neither…nor connects two subjects, the verb agrees with the closer subject ('the junior developers', which is plural), so 'was' is incorrect
'are' is present tense, but the context ('until the last minute') indicates a completed past event, making present tense wrong
'have been' is present perfect, which does not fit the past simple context established by 'until the last minute' referring to a specific completed event

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The new software update not only fixed the previous errors ___ added several features that users had requested for months.
distractor_rationale: 'not only…but' without 'also' is an incomplete correlative structure; the standard form requires 'but also' to balance the two parallel clauses
'not only…and' is not a standard correlative pair; 'and' alone cannot complete the 'not only' structure
'or also' is not a grammatical correlative conjunction and produces a nonsensical sentence when combined with 'not only'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Neither the long journey nor the difficult weather conditions ___ able to stop the team from completing the charity walk.
distractor_rationale: With neither…nor, the verb agrees with the closer subject; 'the difficult weather conditions' is plural, so many learners choose 'were', but the closer subject here is actually 'the difficult weather conditions' — wait: the closer subject IS plural, so 'were' would be expected by proximity rule. However, 'weather conditions' as a collective concept can take singular in formal style. Rewritten: the closer subject is 'the difficult weather conditions' (plural), making 'were' defensible. Item reconstructed below.
present perfect does not fit the completed past event described in the sentence
present tense contradicts the past context of the completed charity walk

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She can speak both French or Spanish fluently.
content.error_sentence: She can speak both French or Spanish fluently.
content.corrected_sentence: She can speak both French and Spanish fluently.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Neither Tom or his sister was at home when we arrived.
content.error_sentence: Neither Tom or his sister was at home when we arrived.
content.corrected_sentence: Neither Tom nor his sister was at home when we arrived.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: You can either stay at the hotel or you can both sleep at our house.
content.correct_answer: or you can sleep at our house
content.error_sentence: You can either stay at the hotel or you can both sleep at our house.
content.corrected_sentence: You can either stay at the hotel or you can sleep at our house.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The new assistant is not only hardworking but accurate as well, which makes her ideal for the role.
content.correct_answer: not only hardworking but also accurate
content.error_sentence: The new assistant is not only hardworking but accurate as well, which makes her ideal for the role.
content.corrected_sentence: The new assistant is not only hardworking but also accurate, which makes her ideal for the role.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Neither the manager or the team members have seen the updated report yet.
content.correct_answer: Neither the manager nor the team members
content.error_sentence: Neither the manager or the team members have seen the updated report yet.
content.corrected_sentence: Neither the manager nor the team members have seen the updated report yet.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Neither the doctor nor the nurses was available to speak to the patient's family at that moment.
content.error_sentence: Neither the doctor nor the nurses was available to speak to the patient's family at that moment.
content.corrected_sentence: Neither the doctor nor the nurses were available to speak to the patient's family at that moment.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The course is designed not only to improve your writing skills but to build your confidence in speaking as well.
content.error_sentence: The course is designed not only to improve your writing skills but to build your confidence in speaking as well.
content.corrected_sentence: The course is designed not only to improve your writing skills but also to build your confidence in speaking as well.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Neither the early morning flight or the afternoon train will get us to the city in time for the meeting.
content.correct_answer: Neither the early morning flight nor the afternoon train
content.error_sentence: Neither the early morning flight or the afternoon train will get us to the city in time for the meeting.
content.corrected_sentence: Neither the early morning flight nor the afternoon train will get us to the city in time for the meeting.
